title: Combination Sum II
description: Given a collection of candidate numbers (candidates) and a target number (target), find all unique combinations in candidates where the candidate numbers sum to target. Each number in candidates may only be used once in the combination. Note: The solution set must not contain duplicate combinations.
thinking_questions: Each element can only be used once. How does the start index change vs Combination Sum I?
With duplicates, how do you avoid generating duplicate combinations?
How do you combine the no-reuse and skip-duplicates logic?

hints.L1: Like Combination Sum I but no reuse (pass i+1). Like Subsets II — sort and skip duplicate elements at same recursion level.
hints.L2: Sort. For each i from start: skip if i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1]. Recurse with i+1 (no reuse). Prune if candidate > remaining.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: no reuse (i+1) + skip duplicates (i>start check)
candidates.sort()
result = []
def backtrack(start, current, remaining):
    if remaining == 0: result.append(current[:]); return
    for i in range(start, len(candidates)):
        if i > start and candidates[i] == candidates[i-1]: continue
        if candidates[i] > remaining: break
        current.append(candidates[i])
        backtrack(i+1, current, remaining - candidates[i])
        current.pop()
backtrack(0, [], target)
return result

follow_up_questions: What are the two differences from Combination Sum I?
Why can you still use the early break here?
How does this compare to Subsets II — what's the additional constraint?
solution_notes: Sort + i+1 (no reuse) + i>start dup-skip + early break. O(2^n).
